scenarios.subject: OVERDUE: Tuition payment of $4,250 — Hold placed on your account
scenarios.body:
Dear Student,

Our records show your Spring 2026 tuition balance of $4,250 is overdue.

A registration hold has been placed on your account. You will be unable to register for Fall classes until paid.

Pay Now → http://yourschool-billing.com/pay

Avoid late fees by paying within 48 hours.

Office of the Bursar
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real universities use their own .edu domains for billing, not generic '.com' addresses like 'yourschool-billing.com'. Real tuition payments are made through your student portal (which you access by logging in to the school's website directly), not via emailed links.

scenarios.subject: Library book due reminder: 'Introduction to Algorithms'
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Your library book 'Introduction to Algorithms' is due in 3 days (June 17, 2026).

You may renew online via the library portal or return it to any campus library branch.

Late fees: $0.25 per day.

University Library
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate library reminder. The sender is @yourschool.edu (the real university domain), the message is factual and non-urgent, contains no clickable suspicious links, and the small late-fee amount is realistic.

scenarios.subject: FAFSA Verification Required — Your aid may be canceled
scenarios.body:
Dear Student,

Your 2026-2027 FAFSA application requires additional verification.

Failure to verify within 7 days will result in your financial aid being canceled.

Verify Now → http://studentaid-verify.gov.co/verify

You will need your SSN and tax documents.

Federal Student Aid
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. The real Federal Student Aid domain is studentaid.gov, not 'studentaid-verify.gov.co' (note the .gov.co — that's a Colombia country code, not the US government). The scam wants your SSN and tax info for identity theft.
redFlags: Fake government domain (.gov.co is not US government)
Asks for SSN via external link
Real FAFSA verification happens at studentaid.gov

scenarios.subject: Your student account will be locked in 24 hours
scenarios.body:
Dear User,

Your student email account has not been verified in the last 90 days.

Verify now to avoid account lock:

Verify Account → http://yourschool-it-support.com/verify

Locked accounts lose access to email, Canvas, and the library portal.

IT Help Desk
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real university IT uses .edu domains. Universities don't lock accounts for not 'verifying' them every 90 days. The link is a credential-stealing page designed to look like your school's login portal.

scenarios.subject: Your Fall 2026 class schedule is now available
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Your Fall 2026 class schedule is now available in the student portal.

Classes:
• CS 380 — Computer Networks
• MATH 250 — Linear Algebra
• ENGL 220 — Modern Literature

Log in to the student portal at yourschool.edu to view full details.

Office of the Registrar
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate registrar notification. The sender is @yourschool.edu (the real university domain), provides factual schedule info, and directs you to log in to the student portal directly without external links.

scenarios.subject: You have been awarded a $5,000 scholarship — Claim today
scenarios.body:
Congratulations!

Based on your academic profile, you have been selected for a $5,000 scholarship.

To claim, provide:
• SSN for tax reporting
• Bank account for direct deposit
• Copy of student ID

Claim Scholarship → http://scholarship-foundation.net/claim

This award expires in 72 hours.

Scholarship Foundation
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real scholarships don't appear out of nowhere with 72-hour deadlines and SSN requests. The 'foundation' is fake; the goal is identity theft and bank account theft. Real scholarships go through your school's financial aid office, not unknown websites.
redFlags: Real scholarships go through financial aid

scenarios.subject: FINAL EXAM ANSWERS — for my CS 380 students only
scenarios.body:
Hi class,

I have shared the final exam answers as a study guide for my CS 380 students.

Download Answers → http://yourschool-prof.com/cs380-answers.zip

Keep this confidential.

Prof. Smith
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real professors use @yourschool.edu addresses, not random '.com' domains. No professor sends 'exam answers' as a study aid — this scam plays on the temptation to cheat. The download link almost certainly contains malware.
redFlags: No real professor distributes exam answers

scenarios.subject: Course registration opens June 20 at 8:00 AM
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Course registration for Fall 2026 opens on Monday, June 20 at 8:00 AM.

Your registration time: 8:00 AM (priority registration based on credit hours).

Log in to the student portal at yourschool.edu to register.

Office of the Registrar
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate registration announcement. The sender is @yourschool.edu (the real university domain), the information is factual, and the directive is to use the student portal — no clickable suspicious links.

scenarios.subject: Unpaid parking citation: $85 — Vehicle may be booted
scenarios.body:
Dear Student,

A parking citation issued on June 10, 2026 remains unpaid.

Citation #: PK-2026-9824
Amount: $85.00

If not paid in 7 days, your vehicle will be booted and a $200 fee added.

Pay Citation → http://campus-parking-services.com/pay

Campus Parking Services
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real campus parking systems use .edu domains or known university vendor portals. The 'boot threat' is meant to scare you into paying without verifying. Real citations are also visible on the actual parking portal you've used before.

scenarios.subject: Your COVID-19 vaccination record has expired — Update required
scenarios.body:
Dear Student,

Our records show your COVID-19 vaccination has expired.

Upload your updated vaccination record within 5 days or your registration will be suspended:

Upload Record → http://yourschool-health-services.com/upload

Student Health Services
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real student health uses .edu domains. Vaccination records don't 'expire' in this generic way, and real schools provide multiple ways to update health records — never just an external upload link from a non-.edu domain.
redFlags: Health info uploaded to unknown domain

scenarios.subject: Career Fair on June 25 — Register now
scenarios.body:
Hi,

The Career Center's Spring Career Fair is on June 25, 2026 from 10 AM - 3 PM in the Main Hall.

50+ companies attending including Google, Stripe, and Salesforce.

Register and view the company list at yourschool.edu/careers.

Career Center
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Career Center announcement. The sender is @yourschool.edu (the real university domain), provides factual event details, and directs you to the official university page without external links.

scenarios.subject: Final reminder: Honor your alma mater with a small donation
scenarios.body:
Dear Alumnus,

We haven't received your annual alumni donation this year.

Even a small gift of $50 helps current students:

Donate Now → http://yourschool-alumni.org/donate

We accept credit card, Bitcoin, and wire transfer.

Alumni Relations
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real alumni fundraising uses .edu domains. Accepting 'Bitcoin' as a donation method for a university is a major red flag. The scam targets recent grads with guilt-based language and a fake donation page that steals card details.
redFlags: Bitcoin payment option (unusual for legit charity)

scenarios.subject: Easy on-campus job: $25/hour — No experience needed
scenarios.body:
Hi,

We have an immediate on-campus opening:

Position: Personal Assistant
Pay: $25/hour, 5 hours/week
Duties: Buy supplies for a busy professor

To start, you'll receive a check to deposit, then send funds to a vendor.

Apply Now → http://campus-employment-portal.com/apply

Campus Jobs
scenarios.explanation: This is a classic 'fake check' scam targeting students. They send you a fake check, you deposit it, send 'vendor' money from your real account, and the original check bounces — leaving you on the hook. Real campus jobs are posted through your school's career portal at .edu domains.

scenarios.subject: Research opportunity in my lab — Summer 2026
scenarios.body:
Hi,

I saw your strong performance in CS 240 and wanted to reach out about a summer research opportunity in my lab.

We're studying distributed systems and looking for one undergraduate research assistant.

If interested, reply with your availability and we can chat during office hours.

Prof. Chen
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate research opportunity from a professor. The sender is @yourschool.edu (the real university domain), the message is conversational and references specific coursework, and asks you to reply via email — no external links or payment requests.

scenarios.subject: Action Required: Verify your transcript request
scenarios.body:
Hello,

We received your request for an official transcript. To complete the request, please verify your identity and pay the $15 processing fee:

Verify & Pay → http://official-transcripts.net/verify

Unverified requests are canceled in 5 days.

Official Transcripts
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real transcript requests go through your university's registrar portal (or a known vendor like Parchment that the school links from its own site). The 'verify and pay' link with a small fee is the classic phishing pattern — small charge to bypass suspicion.
redFlags: Triggered without you actually requesting a transcript

scenarios.subject: Prof. Lee invited you to a Zoom meeting: Office Hours
scenarios.body:
Prof. Lee has invited you to a Zoom meeting.

Topic: Office Hours
Time: June 15, 2026 4:00 PM EST

Join Meeting → http://zoom-meetings-invite.com/join/8472

Password required at join.

Zoom
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Zoom's real domains are zoom.us and zoom.com — not zoom-meetings-invite.com. Real Zoom invites also link to specific meeting URLs on zoom.us. The 'password required' is a credential-stealing trap.
redFlags: Generic invite without specific meeting URL

scenarios.subject: Office hours change for next week
scenarios.body:
Hi class,

My office hours next week are moved:

New time: Tuesday 2-4 PM (instead of Wednesday)
Location: Same — room 314, Math building

See you there.

Prof. Kim
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate office-hours update. The sender is @yourschool.edu (the real university domain), the message is conversational, contains no suspicious links, and the content matches normal professor communication.

scenarios.subject: Internship offer: $30/hour at Goldman Sachs — Apply now
scenarios.body:
Dear Student,

You have been pre-selected for a paid summer internship:

Company: Goldman Sachs
Pay: $30/hour
Duration: 10 weeks

To accept, complete the application and pay the $50 background check fee:

Accept Offer → http://yourschool-internships.com/accept

Only 5 spots remaining.

University Internship Office
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real internships never require applicants to pay an upfront 'background check' fee. The fake scarcity ('only 5 spots') and big company name are bait. Real internship offers come through your school's career services on a .edu domain.

scenarios.subject: Secure your dorm with a $300 deposit — Fall 2026
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Dorm assignments for Fall 2026 are filling up quickly. Secure your spot with a $300 deposit:

Pay Deposit → http://yourschool-housing-office.com/deposit

Deposits will be applied to your tuition. Pay via credit card or Venmo.

Housing Office
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real housing offices use .edu domains. Accepting Venmo for university deposits is unusual and a sign of a scam — real schools use their official student portal. The urgency and external payment link are classic phishing patterns.
redFlags: Venmo payment unusual for school deposits

scenarios.subject: Spring move-out: Important dates and checklist
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Spring 2026 move-out is approaching. Key info:

• Move-out deadline: May 15, 2026, 5 PM
• Room inspections: May 14-15
• Key return: At RA office

Full checklist at yourschool.edu/housing/move-out.

Housing Office
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate housing announcement. The sender is @yourschool.edu (the real university domain), provides factual move-out info, contains no clickable suspicious links, and directs you to the official housing page.

scenarios.subject: Your free Microsoft 365 for students has been deactivated
scenarios.body:
Hello,

Your free Microsoft 365 Education subscription has been deactivated because we couldn't verify your student status.

Reactivate Now → http://office-students-reactivate.com/reactivate

You will be asked to confirm your student email login.

Microsoft Education
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Microsoft's real education domain is microsoft.com or office.com, not office-students-reactivate.com. The 'reactivate' page is a fake Microsoft login designed to steal your student email credentials — which often unlock many other school systems.

scenarios.subject: You qualify for $20,000 in student loan forgiveness — Apply by Friday
scenarios.body:
Dear Borrower,

Under the new federal program, you qualify for up to $20,000 in student loan forgiveness.

To apply, provide your SSN, FSA ID, and bank info:

Apply Now → http://studentloans-forgiveness.com/apply

Program closes Friday — apply today.

Student Loan Forgiveness Center
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Real federal loan forgiveness is applied for at studentaid.gov — a US government domain. Any service that requires your FSA ID and SSN through an external '.com' is harvesting credentials for identity theft. Real federal programs never charge fees or have weekend-only deadlines.
redFlags: Asks for SSN and FSA ID
Real loan forgiveness goes through studentaid.gov

scenarios.subject: Speaker series: Dr. Hannah Liu on AI ethics, June 22
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Join us for a speaker series event:

Dr. Hannah Liu (Stanford) on 'AI Ethics in 2026'
When: Monday, June 22, 6:00 PM
Where: Wilson Auditorium

Free and open to all students. RSVP at yourschool.edu/events.

Department of Computer Science
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate event announcement. The sender is @yourschool.edu (the real university domain), provides factual event info, and directs you to the official events page — no suspicious links or personal info requests.

scenarios.subject: Suspicious login to your Canvas account from another country
scenarios.body:
Dear Student,

We detected a login attempt to your Canvas account from China.

If this was not you, secure your account immediately:

Secure Canvas → http://canvas-lms-security.net/secure

Your assignments and grades may be at risk.

Canvas Security
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Canvas's real domain is instructure.com (the company that owns Canvas) or your school's .edu Canvas subdomain. The scary 'foreign country' login is meant to panic you. The 'secure account' link is a credential-stealing page.
redFlags: Real Canvas logins are checked at instructure.com or your school's portal

scenarios.subject: End-of-semester course evaluations are open
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Course evaluations for Spring 2026 are now open.

Your feedback helps improve courses and faculty. Evaluations are anonymous.

Complete evaluations at yourschool.edu/evaluations by May 10.

Thank you,
Office of Institutional Research
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate course evaluation reminder. The sender is @yourschool.edu (the real university domain), the message is factual, contains no clickable suspicious links, and directs you to the official evaluation page.
